content:
# Numbers Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), p. 162; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 121 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_012_page_001.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — MIRIAM AND AARON AGAINST MOSES: THE CRISIS OF PROPHETIC AUTHORITY:** Numbers 12 is one of the most tightly constructed narratives in the Torah — sixteen verses that move from sibling accusation to divine theophany to leprous judgment to intercessory healing. The chapter divides into four movements: the complaint of Miriam and Aaron against Moses (vv. 1–3), YHWH's sudden summons and rebuke (vv. 4–8), YHWH's anger and Miriam's leprosy (vv. 9–12), and Moses' intercession and Miriam's seven-day exclusion (vv. 13–16). The episode is set at Khatserot (Hazeroth), where the camp has arrived at the close of chapter 11. On the Leningrad Codex page 162, this chapter begins in the left column following the setumah (ס) that closes chapter 11. The Vaticanus preserves the chapter on page 121, following closely upon the quail narrative. The singular feminine verb וַתְּדַבֵּר in v. 1 ("and she spoke") identifies Miriam as the instigator, though Aaron joins the complaint. The chapter's theological center is YHWH's speech in vv. 6–8, which establishes Moses' prophetic uniqueness: while other prophets receive visions and dreams, Moses alone speaks with YHWH "mouth to mouth" (פֶּה אֶל־פֶּה), beholding the "form" (תְּמוּנָה) of YHWH.

---

## The Complaint of Miriam and Aaron (vv. 1–3)

**1** And-spoke [va-tedabber] Miriam [Miryam] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] against [be-] Moses [Mosheh] on-account of [al-odot] the-Cushite [ha-Kushit] woman [ha-ishah] whom [asher] he-had-taken [laqakh]; for [ki] a-Cushite [Kushit] woman [ishah] he-had-taken [laqakh].

> **Key terms:** וַתְּדַבֵּר (va-tedabber) = "and she spoke" — the verb is third-person feminine singular (piel wayyiqtol of דבר), identifying Miriam as the primary speaker, though Aaron is named alongside her. The preposition בְּ (be-) with דבר means "to speak against" — a hostile usage (cf. Num 21:5, 7; Ps 50:20). עַל־אֹדוֹת (al-odot) = "on account of / concerning" — the preposition introduces the subject of the complaint: Moses' Cushite wife. הָאִשָּׁה הַכֻּשִׁית (ha-ishah ha-Kushit) = "the Cushite woman" — כּוּשׁ (Kush) typically refers to the region south of Egypt (modern Sudan/Ethiopia). The identity of this woman is debated: she may be Zipporah (if "Cushite" is interpreted broadly as "dark-skinned foreigner" or as Midianite), or she may be a second wife taken after Zipporah. The clause כִּי־אִשָּׁה כֻשִׁית לָקָח (ki ishah Kushit laqakh) = "for a Cushite woman he had taken" — the repetition emphasizes the foreignness of the wife as the ostensible grounds of complaint. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν Μαριαμ καὶ Ααρων κατὰ Μωυσῆ ἕνεκεν τῆς γυναικὸς τῆς Αἰθιοπίσσης ἣν ἔλαβεν Μωυσῆς ("and Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses on account of the Ethiopian woman whom Moses had taken") — the LXX renders כֻּשִׁית as Αἰθιοπίσσης (Aithiopissēs, "Ethiopian woman"), its standard equivalent.

**2** And-they-said [va-yomeru]: "Is-it-only [ha-raq] through [be-] Moses [Mosheh] [that] spoke [dibber] YHWH? Has-not [ha-lo] also [gam] through-us [banu] he-spoke [dibber]?" And-heard [va-yishma] YHWH.

> **Key terms:** הֲרַק אַךְ בְּמֹשֶׁה דִּבֶּר יְהוָה (ha-raq akh be-Mosheh dibber YHWH) = "Is it only through Moses [that] YHWH spoke?" — the particles הֲרַק (ha-raq, "is it only?") introduce a rhetorical question challenging Moses' exclusive prophetic role. The complaint has shifted from the Cushite wife (v. 1) to prophetic authority (v. 2) — the wife was the pretext; the real grievance is Moses' unique status. הֲלֹא גַּם־בָּנוּ דִבֵּר (ha-lo gam banu dibber) = "has he not also spoken through us?" — Miriam is a prophetess (Exod 15:20) and Aaron is the priestly spokesman (Exod 4:14–16), so their claim has some basis — but it ignores the qualitative difference YHWH will articulate in vv. 6–8. וַיִּשְׁמַע יְהוָה (va-yishma YHWH) = "and YHWH heard" — this terse clause, standing alone at the end of the verse, signals that YHWH has taken notice and will respond. The same construction appears in 11:1, where YHWH's hearing triggers immediate anger. **LXX:** μὴ Μωυσῇ μόνῳ ἐλάλησεν κύριος; οὐχὶ καὶ ἡμῖν ἐλάλησεν; καὶ ἤκουσεν κύριος ("has [the] Lord spoken to Moses alone? Has he not also spoken to us? And [the] Lord heard").

**3** And-the-man [ve-ha-ish] Moses [Mosheh] [was] very [me'od] humble [anav], more-than-all [mi-kol] the-man [ha-adam] who [asher] [was] upon [al] the-face of [penei] the-ground [ha-adamah].

> **Key terms:** עָנָו (anav) = "humble/meek/afflicted" — from the root ענה (anah, "to be bowed down, afflicted, humble"). The word carries the sense of one who does not assert his own rights or status. מִכֹּל הָאָדָם אֲשֶׁר עַל־פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה (mi-kol ha-adam asher al penei ha-adamah) = "more than all the man/humanity that [was] upon the face of the ground" — a superlative construction: Moses was the most humble person on earth. This parenthetical narrator's comment explains why Moses did not defend himself against Miriam and Aaron's accusation — his humility prevented self-advocacy. The verse is often noted as a difficulty: if Moses wrote the Torah, how could he call himself the most humble man alive? The traditional answer (Talmud, Shabbat 89a; and others) is that Moses wrote what YHWH dictated, including this assessment. **LXX:** καὶ ὁ ἄνθρωπος Μωυσῆς πραΰς σφόδρα παρὰ πάντας τοὺς ἀνθρώπους τοὺς ὄντας ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ("and the man Moses [was] very gentle/meek beyond all the men who were upon the earth") — rendering עָנָו with πραΰς (praus, "meek/gentle"), the same word used in Matthew 5:5 ("Blessed are the meek").

---

## YHWH's Summons and Rebuke (vv. 4–8)

**4** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH suddenly [pit'om] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon] and-unto [ve-el] Miriam [Miryam]: "Go-out [tse'u], [you] three of you [sheloshtekhem], unto [el] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed]." And-went-out [va-yets'u] [the] three of them [sheloshtam].

> **Key terms:** פִּתְאֹם (pit'om) = "suddenly/unexpectedly" — the adverb conveys the startling immediacy of YHWH's intervention. YHWH does not wait; the response is abrupt and dramatic. שְׁלָשְׁתְּכֶם (sheloshtekhem) = "three of you" — all three siblings are summoned: Moses the prophet, Aaron the priest, Miriam the prophetess. The command צְאוּ (tse'u) = "go out" — imperative masculine plural of יצא. The destination is the Tent of Meeting, the site of divine encounter. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος παραχρῆμα πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων καὶ Μαριαμ· ἐξέλθατε ὑμεῖς οἱ τρεῖς εἰς τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and [the] Lord said immediately to Moses and Aaron and Miriam: go out, you three, to the tent of witness") — παραχρῆμα (parakhrēma, "immediately/at once") for פִּתְאֹם.

**5** And-came-down [va-yered] YHWH in-a-pillar of [be-ammud] cloud [anan], and-stood [va-ya'amod] [at]-the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent [ha-Ohel]; and-called [va-yiqra] Aaron [Aharon] and-Miriam [u-Miryam]; and-went-out [va-yets'u] both-of-them [shneihem].

> **Key terms:** בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן (be-ammud anan) = "in a pillar of cloud" — the standard medium of YHWH's theophanic presence (cf. Exod 33:9–10; Num 11:25). The cloud descends to the entrance of the Tent — YHWH comes to meet them, not the reverse. וַיִּקְרָא אַהֲרֹן וּמִרְיָם (va-yiqra Aharon u-Miryam) = "and he called Aaron and Miriam" — only the two accusers are summoned forward; Moses is not called because he is not on trial. וַיֵּצְאוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם (va-yets'u shneihem) = "and the two of them went out" — the dual number isolates Aaron and Miriam before the divine presence for rebuke. **LXX:** καὶ κατέβη κύριος ἐν στύλῳ νεφέλης καὶ ἔστη ἐπὶ τῆς θύρας τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου· καὶ ἐκλήθησαν Ααρων καὶ Μαριαμ ("and [the] Lord came down in a pillar of cloud and stood at the door of the tent of witness; and Aaron and Miriam were called") — the LXX uses a passive form ἐκλήθησαν ("were called") where the MT has the active וַיִּקְרָא ("and he called").

**6** And-he-said [va-yomer]: "Hear [shim'u] now [na] my-words [devarai]: If [im] there-shall-be [yihyeh] your-prophet [nevi'akhem], [I] YHWH in-the-vision [ba-mar'ah] unto-him [elav] I-make-myself-known [etvadda]; in-the-dream [ba-khalom] I-speak [adabber] to-him [bo].

> **Key terms:** שִׁמְעוּ־נָא דְבָרָי (shim'u na devarai) = "hear now my words" — the imperative with נָא (na, "please/now") opens YHWH's formal declaration. נְבִיאֲכֶם (nevi'akhem) = "your prophet" — i.e., a prophet among you, or a prophet of your [caliber]. The possessive suffix addresses Miriam and Aaron: "if there should be a prophet of yours." בַּמַּרְאָה (ba-mar'ah) = "in the vision" — from ראה (ra'ah, "to see"); a מַרְאָה is a visionary experience, a visual revelation. אֶתְוַדַּע (etvadda) = "I make myself known" — the hitpael of ידע (yada', "to know"), expressing YHWH's self-disclosure. בַּחֲלוֹם (ba-khalom) = "in the dream" — חֲלוֹם (khalom) is the standard medium of prophetic communication (cf. Gen 20:3; 28:12; 1 Kgs 3:5). YHWH communicates with ordinary prophets through two media: visions and dreams. **LXX:** ἐὰν γένηται προφήτης ὑμῶν κυρίῳ, ἐν ὁράματι αὐτῷ γνωσθήσομαι, καὶ ἐν ὕπνῳ λαλήσω αὐτῷ ("if there shall be a prophet of yours to [the] Lord, in a vision I shall be known to him, and in sleep I shall speak to him") — rendering חֲלוֹם ("dream") as ὕπνος (hupnos, "sleep"), a slight shift from the specific to the general.

**7** Not [lo] so [khen] my-servant [avdi] Moses [Mosheh]; in-all [be-khol] my-house [beiti] faithful [ne'eman] he [hu].

> **Key terms:** לֹא־כֵן עַבְדִּי מֹשֶׁה (lo khen avdi Mosheh) = "not so my servant Moses" — the emphatic negation distinguishes Moses from all other prophets. עַבְדִּי (avdi) = "my servant" — the title of supreme intimacy in the Hebrew Bible, denoting one who stands in direct service to YHWH (cf. Josh 1:2; Isa 42:1). בְּכָל־בֵּיתִי נֶאֱמָן הוּא (be-khol beiti ne'eman hu) = "in all my house faithful [is] he" — בַּיִת (bayit, "house") here means YHWH's entire household, i.e., Israel and the tabernacle economy. נֶאֱמָן (ne'eman) = "faithful/trustworthy" — the niphal participle of אמן (aman, "to be firm/reliable"), the root underlying אֱמֶת (emet, "truth") and אָמֵן (amen). Moses is not merely a prophet but the trusted steward of YHWH's entire house. The allusion to stewardship (cf. Heb 3:2–6, which explicitly cites this verse) establishes Moses' role as qualitatively different from all other servants of YHWH. **LXX:** οὐχ οὕτως ὁ θεράπων μου Μωυσῆς· ἐν ὅλῳ τῷ οἴκῳ μου πιστός ἐστιν ("not so my servant Moses; in all my house faithful he is") — θεράπων (therapōn, "attendant/servant") is the distinctive LXX title for Moses, used also in Hebrews 3:5.

**8** Mouth [peh] unto [el] mouth [peh] I-speak [adabber] to-him [bo], and-[in]-appearance [u-mar'eh] and-not [ve-lo] [in]-riddles [be-khidot]; and-the-form of [u-temunat] YHWH he-beholds [yabbit]. And-why [u-maddua] were-you-not [lo] afraid [yeretem] to-speak [ledabber] against-my-servant [be-avdi], against-Moses [be-Mosheh]?"

> **Key terms:** פֶּה אֶל־פֶּה (peh el peh) = "mouth to mouth" — the most intimate possible mode of communication, face-to-face speech without mediation. This contrasts with the "visions" and "dreams" of v. 6. וּמַרְאֶה (u-mar'eh) = "and [in] appearance/sight" — from ראה; Moses sees YHWH directly rather than through encrypted symbols. וְלֹא בְחִידֹת (ve-lo be-khidot) = "and not in riddles" — חִידָה (khidah, "riddle/enigma") refers to obscure, encoded speech requiring interpretation (cf. Judg 14:12–18; Prov 1:6). Other prophets receive coded messages; Moses receives plain speech. וּתְמֻנַת יְהוָה יַבִּיט (u-temunat YHWH yabbit) = "and the form of YHWH he beholds" — תְּמוּנָה (temunah, "form/likeness/figure") is the same word used in the second commandment (Deut 4:12, 15–16) to describe what Israel did not see at Sinai. Moses alone sees the temunah of YHWH — a staggering claim that sets him apart from all other prophets and from Israel as a whole. The verb יַבִּיט (yabbit) = "he beholds/gazes upon" — the hiphil of נבט (navat), implying sustained, contemplative seeing. The final question — וּמַדּוּעַ לֹא יְרֵאתֶם לְדַבֵּר בְּעַבְדִּי בְמֹשֶׁה (u-maddua lo yeretem ledabber be-avdi be-Mosheh) = "and why were you not afraid to speak against my servant, against Moses?" — is devastating: YHWH holds Miriam and Aaron accountable not merely for error but for a failure of fear. **LXX:** στόμα κατὰ στόμα λαλήσω αὐτῷ, ἐν εἴδει καὶ οὐ δι᾽ αἰνιγμάτων, καὶ τὴν δόξαν κυρίου εἶδεν ("mouth to mouth I shall speak to him, in visible form and not through enigmas, and the glory of [the] Lord he saw") — notably, the LXX renders תְּמוּנַת יְהוָה ("form of YHWH") as τὴν δόξαν κυρίου ("the glory of the Lord"), substituting δόξα (doxa, "glory") for the more concrete "form," perhaps to protect divine transcendence.

---

## YHWH's Anger and Miriam's Leprosy (vv. 9–12)

**9** And-burned [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] YHWH against-them [bam], and-he-departed [va-yelakh].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּחַר אַף יְהוָה בָּם (va-yikhar af YHWH bam) = "and the anger of YHWH burned against them" — the standard idiom for divine wrath (cf. 11:1, 10, 33). The suffix בָּם (bam, "against them") is plural, indicating that YHWH's anger falls on both Miriam and Aaron, though only Miriam is subsequently struck with leprosy. וַיֵּלַךְ (va-yelakh) = "and he departed" — YHWH's departure is itself the judgment: the withdrawal of the divine presence leaves them exposed. The brevity of this verse — only six Hebrew words — conveys the swiftness and finality of YHWH's response. **LXX:** καὶ ὀργὴ θυμοῦ κυρίου ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῖς, καὶ ἀπῆλθεν ("and [the] wrath of [the] anger of [the] Lord [was] upon them, and he departed") — the LXX doubles the anger vocabulary with ὀργὴ θυμοῦ ("wrath of fury"), intensifying the MT's single אַף ("anger").

**10** And-the-cloud [ve-he-anan] departed [sar] from-upon [me-al] the-Tent [ha-Ohel]; and-behold [ve-hinneh], Miriam [Miryam] [was] leprous [metsora'at] as-snow [ka-shaleg]; and-turned [va-yifen] Aaron [Aharon] unto [el] Miriam [Miryam], and-behold [ve-hinneh] [she was] leprous [metsora'at].

> **Key terms:** וְהֶעָנָן סָר מֵעַל הָאֹהֶל (ve-he-anan sar me-al ha-Ohel) = "and the cloud departed from upon the Tent" — the cloud's removal signals YHWH's withdrawal. The verb סָר (sar) = "turned aside/departed" — from סור (sur). מְצֹרַעַת כַּשָּׁלֶג (metsora'at ka-shaleg) = "leprous as snow" — the feminine form of מְצֹרָע (metsora, "one afflicted with tsara'at"). The comparison כַּשָּׁלֶג (ka-shaleg, "as/like snow") describes the whiteness of the affliction (cf. Exod 4:6, where Moses' hand becomes "leprous as snow"; 2 Kgs 5:27, where Gehazi becomes "leprous as snow"). צָרַעַת (tsara'at) is a skin condition rendering the afflicted ritually impure — it is not identical to modern leprosy (Hansen's disease) but encompasses various severe skin disorders. The double וְהִנֵּה (ve-hinneh, "and behold") — first by the narrator and then through Aaron's eyes — dramatizes the shock of discovery. **LXX:** καὶ ἡ νεφέλη ἀπέστη ἀπὸ τῆς σκηνῆς, καὶ ἰδοὺ Μαριαμ λεπρῶσα ὡσεὶ χιών ("and the cloud departed from the tent, and behold Miriam [was] leprous as snow") — λεπρῶσα (leprōsa) for מְצֹרַעַת.

**11** And-said [va-yomer] Aaron [Aharon] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Please [bi], my-lord [adoni], do-not [al na] lay [tasheth] upon-us [aleinu] sin [khatta'at] in-which [asher] we-were-foolish [no'alnu] and-in-which [va-asher] we-sinned [khatanu].

> **Key terms:** בִּי אֲדֹנִי (bi adoni) = "please, my lord" — the particle בִּי is an entreaty/plea (literally "in me"), and אֲדֹנִי (adoni, "my lord") is a title of deference. Aaron addresses Moses as a subordinate to a superior — a dramatic reversal from the claim of equal prophetic status in v. 2. אַל־נָא תָשֵׁת עָלֵינוּ חַטָּאת (al na tasheth aleinu khatta'at) = "do not, please, lay upon us sin" — the verb שׁית (shit, "to set/place/lay") with חַטָּאת (khatta'at, "sin/sin-offering") means "do not charge us with [this] sin." נוֹאַלְנוּ (no'alnu) = "we were foolish/acted foolishly" — from יאל (ya'al), niphal, "to be foolish, to show oneself a fool" (cf. Isa 19:13; Jer 5:4). Aaron confesses both folly and sin: the foolishness of presumption and the sin of speaking against YHWH's chosen servant. **LXX:** δέομαι, κύριε, μὴ συνεπιθῇ ἡμῖν ἁμαρτίαν, διότι ἠγνοήσαμεν καθότι ἡμάρτομεν ("I beseech, lord, do not lay sin upon us, because we acted in ignorance inasmuch as we sinned") — rendering נוֹאַלְנוּ with ἠγνοήσαμεν (ēgnoēsamen, "we were ignorant"), interpreting foolishness as ignorance.

**12** Let-her-not [al na] be [tehi] as-the-dead [ka-met], who [asher] when-he-comes-out [be-tse'to] from-the-womb of [me-rekhem] his-mother [immo], and-is-consumed [va-ye'akhel] half [khatsi] his-flesh [besaro]."

> **Key terms:** אַל־נָא תְהִי כַּמֵּת (al na tehi ka-met) = "let her not, please, be as the dead [one]" — Aaron pleads that Miriam not remain in her leprous condition, which he compares to a stillborn child. כַּמֵּת (ka-met) = "as the dead" — the מֵת (met, "dead one") refers to a corpse or stillborn. אֲשֶׁר בְּצֵאתוֹ מֵרֶחֶם אִמּוֹ (asher be-tse'to me-rekhem immo) = "who when he comes out from the womb of his mother" — the stillborn emerges from the womb already decayed. וַיֵּאָכֵל חֲצִי בְשָׂרוֹ (va-ye'akhel khatsi besaro) = "and half his flesh is consumed" — the niphal of אכל (akhal, "to eat/consume"), describing a body whose flesh has been half-eaten away. The image is horrifying: Miriam's leprous white skin looks like a partially decomposed stillborn infant. Aaron's comparison drives the urgency of the plea — his sister is becoming a living corpse. **LXX:** μὴ γένηται ὡσεὶ ἴσον θανάτῳ, ὡσεὶ ἔκτρωμα ἐκπορευόμενον ἐκ μήτρας μητρός, καὶ κατεσθίει τὸ ἥμισυ τῶν σαρκῶν αὐτῆς ("let her not be as one equal to death, as a miscarriage coming forth from [the] womb of [a] mother, and it consumes half of her flesh") — the LXX adds ἔκτρωμα (ektrōma, "miscarriage/abortion"), making explicit what the MT implies.

---

## Moses' Intercession and Miriam's Exclusion (vv. 13–16)

**13** And-cried [va-yits'aq] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] YHWH, saying [le'mor]: "God [El], please [na], heal [refa] please [na] to-her [lah]!"

> **Key terms:** וַיִּצְעַק (va-yits'aq) = "and he cried out" — from צעק (tsa'aq), the same verb used for the people's desperate cry in 11:2. Moses' intercession is immediate and passionate. אֵל נָא רְפָא נָא לָהּ (El na refa na lah) = "God, please, heal please, her" — one of the shortest and most intense prayers in the Hebrew Bible. Five Hebrew words. The double נָא (na, "please") expresses urgent entreaty. רְפָא (refa) = "heal" — the qal imperative of רפא (rafa', "to heal"). The prayer is addressed to אֵל (El, "God"), using the short divine name rather than the full YHWH. The brevity of the prayer — no elaborate petition, no argumentation, no appeal to covenant — reflects both Moses' humility and his intimacy with YHWH: he need say no more. This is the man who was just accused of arrogance, now interceding for his accuser. **LXX:** καὶ ἐβόησεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς κύριον λέγων· ὁ θεός, δέομαί σου, ἴασαι αὐτήν ("and Moses cried out to [the] Lord, saying: O God, I beseech you, heal her").

**14** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "And-if [ve-im] her-father [aviha] had-surely-spit [yaroq yaraq] in-her-face [be-faneiha], would-she-not [ha-lo] be-shamed [tikkalam] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim]? Let-her-be-shut-up [tissager] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim] outside [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh], and-after [ve-akhar] she-shall-be-gathered-in [te'asef]."

> **Key terms:** וְאָבִיהָ יָרֹק יָרַק בְּפָנֶיהָ (ve-aviha yaroq yaraq be-faneiha) = "and [if] her father had surely spit in her face" — the infinitive absolute יָרֹק (yaroq) intensifies the finite verb יָרַק (yaraq): "had utterly spit." Spitting in the face was an act of extreme shame and contempt (cf. Deut 25:9; Isa 50:6; Job 30:10). YHWH uses a qal va-khomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if a human father's spitting warranted seven days of shame, how much more does divine rebuke warrant exclusion? הֲלֹא תִכָּלֵם שִׁבְעַת יָמִים (ha-lo tikkalam shiv'at yamim) = "would she not be shamed seven days?" — the niphal of כלם (kalam, "to be humiliated/put to shame"). תִּסָּגֵר (tissager) = "let her be shut up/confined" — the niphal of סגר (sagar, "to shut/close"), the technical term for quarantine in the tsara'at laws of Leviticus 13–14. The seven-day period mirrors the priestly examination period for skin diseases (Lev 13:4–5). וְאַחַר תֵּאָסֵף (ve-akhar te'asef) = "and after [that] she shall be gathered in" — i.e., readmitted to the camp. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· εἰ ὁ πατὴρ αὐτῆς πτύων ἐνέπτυσεν εἰς τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτῆς, οὐκ ἐντραπήσεται ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας; ἀφορισθήτω ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς ("and [the] Lord said to Moses: if her father had spit [by spitting] in her face, would she not be ashamed seven days? Let her be separated seven days outside the camp") — ἀφορισθήτω (aphoristhētō, "let her be set apart") for תִּסָּגֵר.

**15** And-was-shut-up [va-tissager] Miriam [Miryam] outside [mi-khuts] the-camp [la-makhaneh] seven [shiv'at] days [yamim]; and-the-people [ve-ha-am] did-not [lo] journey [nasa] until [ad] the-gathering-in of [he'asef] Miriam [Miryam].

> **Key terms:** וַתִּסָּגֵר מִרְיָם (va-tissager Miryam) = "and Miriam was shut up" — the niphal of סגר, confirming the execution of YHWH's command from v. 14. מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה (mi-khuts la-makhaneh) = "outside the camp" — the standard place of exclusion for those with tsara'at (cf. Lev 13:46). וְהָעָם לֹא נָסַע עַד הֵאָסֵף מִרְיָם (ve-ha-am lo nasa ad he'asef Miryam) = "and the people did not journey until the gathering-in of Miriam" — the entire congregation of Israel waited for Miriam's restoration before moving on. This detail honors Miriam: despite her sin, the people would not leave her behind. The Talmud (Sotah 9b–10a) sees this as measure-for-measure: Miriam waited by the Nile to watch over the infant Moses (Exod 2:4), and now the whole camp waits for her. **LXX:** καὶ ἀφωρίσθη Μαριαμ ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας· καὶ ὁ λαὸς οὐκ ἐξῆρεν ἕως ἐκαθαρίσθη Μαριαμ ("and Miriam was set apart outside the camp seven days; and the people did not set out until Miriam was cleansed") — notably, the LXX adds ἐκαθαρίσθη (ekatharisthē, "was cleansed/purified") where the MT has הֵאָסֵף ("was gathered in"), making the purification explicit.

**16** And-after [ve-akhar] the-people [ha-am] journeyed [nas'u] from-Khatserot [me-Khatserot]; and-they-camped [va-yakhanu] in-the-wilderness of [be-midbar] Paran [Pa'ran].

> **Key terms:** מֵחֲצֵרוֹת (me-Khatserot) = "from Hazeroth" — the campsite where the entire episode of chapter 12 took place, as established in 11:35. מִדְבַּר פָּארָן (midbar Pa'ran) = "the wilderness of Paran" — the great desert region of the northeastern Sinai/southern Negev, which will serve as the staging area for the spy mission in chapter 13. The itinerary note signals the end of the Khatserot crisis and the transition to the next major episode: the reconnaissance of the land of Canaan. **LXX:** καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα ἐξῆρεν ὁ λαὸς ἐξ Ασηρωθ, καὶ παρενέβαλον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ τοῦ Φαραν ("and after these things the people set out from Haseroth, and encamped in the wilderness of Pharan").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (p. 162)

1. **Chapter location and parashah boundary:** Numbers 12 begins on page 162 of the Leningrad Codex, in the left column of the folio (folio 84 recto), following the setumah (ס) that closes 11:35. The chapter is relatively short (sixteen verses) and fits within this page. The text is written in the standard three-column format of the Leningrad Codex, with Masoretic annotations in the margins and between the columns.

2. **The feminine singular verb וַתְּדַבֵּר (v. 1):** The opening verb is clearly vocalized as a third-person feminine singular piel wayyiqtol in the Leningrad Codex: וַתְּדַבֵּר (va-tedabber, "and she spoke"). This is grammatically significant because both Miriam and Aaron are named as subjects, yet the verb agrees only with Miriam. This construction identifies Miriam as the instigator of the complaint. Had Aaron been the primary agent, the verb would be masculine singular (וַיְדַבֵּר). The feminine form is preserved without correction or qere notation, confirming its intentionality.

3. **The Cushite wife — double clause (v. 1):** The phrase כִּי־אִשָּׁה כֻשִׁית לָקָח (ki ishah Kushit laqakh, "for a Cushite woman he had taken") repeats the information already stated. This repetition is not scribal error — it is deliberate rhetorical emphasis visible in the Leningrad Codex without any sign of dittography or correction. The double mention of the Cushite wife underscores that her ethnic identity is the focal point of the complaint.

4. **The parenthetical of v. 3 — the humility of Moses:** The statement that Moses was "very humble, more than all the man who was upon the face of the ground" stands as a narrator's aside interrupting the narrative flow. In the Leningrad Codex, this verse shows no special formatting — it is continuous with the surrounding text — confirming that the Masoretes treated it as integral to the text, not as a marginal or late gloss.

5. **YHWH's speech (vv. 6–8) — the poetic structure:** The divine speech in vv. 6–8 has a discernibly elevated, quasi-poetic quality in the Hebrew: parallel clauses (vision/dream in v. 6; mouth to mouth / appearance / not in riddles in v. 8) and the rhythmic contrast between ordinary prophets and Moses. The Leningrad Codex does not set this passage in stichometric (poetic) layout — it remains in the prose column format — but the internal parallelism is clear from the text itself.

6. **Moses' prayer — אֵל נָא רְפָא נָא לָהּ (v. 13):** This five-word prayer is one of the most compact intercessions in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex shows no unusual spacing or markings around this verse. The double נָא (na, "please") is clearly written, and the imperative רְפָא (refa, "heal") is vocalized as a qal imperative without ambiguity.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (p. 121)

7. **LXX rendering of תְּמוּנַת יְהוָה as τὴν δόξαν κυρίου (v. 8):** The Vaticanus reads τὴν δόξαν κυρίου ("the glory of the Lord") where the MT has תְּמוּנַת יְהוָה ("the form of YHWH"). This is a theologically significant divergence. The Hebrew תְּמוּנָה (temunah) means "form/shape/likeness" — it implies a visible, quasi-physical manifestation. The LXX translators appear to have replaced "form" with "glory" (δόξα, doxa), either to avoid the anthropomorphism of YHWH having a visible "form" or because they understood the "form" as equivalent to the "glory" (כָּבוֹד, kavod) tradition of Exodus 33:18–23. This substitution softens the bold claim of the MT that Moses directly beholds YHWH's form.

8. **LXX addition of ἔκτρωμα (v. 12):** The Vaticanus inserts ὡσεὶ ἔκτρωμα ("as a miscarriage") in Aaron's plea, making the stillbirth imagery explicit. The MT uses only כַּמֵּת (ka-met, "as the dead [one]"), leaving the reader to infer the stillborn comparison from context. The LXX's addition clarifies the image but also narrows the interpretive range — the MT's "dead one" could theoretically refer to any corpse, while the LXX's "miscarriage" specifies a particular kind of death.

9. **LXX ἐκαθαρίσθη for הֵאָסֵף (v. 15):** The Vaticanus reads ἐκαθαρίσθη (ekatharisthē, "was cleansed") where the MT has הֵאָסֵף (he'asef, "was gathered in"). The MT's "gathering in" is a spatial term — Miriam was readmitted to the camp. The LXX's "was cleansed" is a ritual/cultic term — Miriam was purified. The LXX reading interprets the readmission through the lens of the Leviticus 13–14 purification process, while the MT focuses on the communal reintegration.

### Theological and Structural Notes

10. **The shift from pretext to substance (vv. 1–2):** The chapter opens with two distinct complaints that are poorly harmonized: v. 1 criticizes Moses' Cushite wife; v. 2 challenges Moses' prophetic monopoly. The wife is never mentioned again, and YHWH's response addresses only the prophetic question. This narrative structure suggests that the Cushite wife was the pretext — the surface-level grievance — while the real issue was envy of Moses' unique status. The feminine singular verb (va-tedabber) attributes the initiative to Miriam, and the punishment falls on Miriam alone, suggesting that she was the driving force behind both the pretext and the substantive challenge.

11. **The qal va-khomer in v. 14 — divine logic and human shame:** YHWH's argument follows the rabbinic qal va-khomer (a fortiori) pattern: if a father's spitting warrants seven days of shame, then divine rebuke warrants at least as much. The argument is notable because YHWH does not say "how much more" (as a full qal va-khomer would), but simply prescribes the seven-day minimum — suggesting that Miriam's exclusion is an act of mercy. The divine punishment could have been far greater; instead, it is calibrated to the human analogy. This restrained judgment stands in contrast to the immediate plague at Qivrot ha-Ta'avah (11:33).

12. **Miriam's silence:** Throughout the entire chapter, Miriam never speaks. She initiates the complaint (v. 1), but her actual words are reported as part of the joint statement with Aaron (v. 2). After YHWH's rebuke, she says nothing. Aaron speaks on her behalf (vv. 11–12), and Moses prays for her (v. 13), but Miriam herself is silent from the moment of her affliction to her restoration. This silence may be part of the narrative's portrayal of her humbling — the one who "spoke against" Moses is rendered speechless — or it may suggest the gravity of tsara'at, which placed the afflicted in a state of social death where speech was no longer appropriate.

13. **Moses as intercessor for his accuser:** The chapter's most profound theological moment is v. 13, where Moses — the target of Miriam's complaint — immediately cries out for her healing. This is the inverse of what human nature would predict. Moses does not gloat, does not say "I told you so," does not use the moment to consolidate power. He intercedes. The brevity of his prayer ("God, please, heal please, her!") conveys urgency without any self-reference. This act of intercession vindicates YHWH's description of Moses in v. 3 ("very humble, more than all") and in v. 7 ("in all my house faithful he is"). Moses' faithfulness is demonstrated not by his prophetic gifts but by his willingness to pray for those who have wronged him — a pattern that will recur in Numbers 14:13–19, 16:22, and 21:7.

14. **The camp waits for Miriam (v. 15):** The detail that the entire people did not journey until Miriam was gathered back into the camp is a remarkable narrative gesture. The cloud has presumably not moved (since the people could not travel without it), but the text emphasizes the human solidarity: all Israel waited. The Talmud (Sotah 9b) reads this as a reciprocal honor — Miriam once waited by the Nile to see what would become of the infant Moses (Exod 2:4), and now the whole congregation waits by Khatserot to see what will become of Miriam. Whether or not this intertextual connection is original to the author's intention, the narrative effect is clear: judgment is not the last word. Miriam is disciplined, but she is not abandoned.
